text: Following adhesiolysis and reduction of the hernia sac, the surgeon begins dissecting into the preperitoneal plane at the midline, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the tissue layers.

The surgeon begins dissection in the contralateral rectus muscle plane to address a concomitant midline hernia, aiming to achieve sufficient mesh overlap after closing the midline hernia during the peristomal hernia repair.

The surgeon continues dissection in the preperitoneal plane, encountering some difficulty due to the presence of mesh and tackers. After crossing the midline, the dissection progresses into the retromuscular plane.
The surgeon dissects the retromuscular plane, revealing that the presence of the IPOM mesh and absorbable tackers does not significantly impede the dissection process.

The surgeon encounters difficulty while dissecting the retromuscular plane surrounding the stoma due to the ingrowth of mesh into the bowel, which visually complicates the separation of these fused structures.

The surgeon continues dissection into the lower abdomen, passing the arcuate line where the posterior rectus fascia is absent, and resumes dissection in the preperitoneal plane. The mesh ingrowth visibly hampers the dissection process.
The surgeon develops the recto-rectus plane surrounding the stoma and divides the plane towards the stoma, allowing the tissue flaps to hang downwards and improve visibility around the stoma.

The surgeon encounters difficulty while dissecting the plane around the stoma due to the mesh being tightly adhered to the bowel. Part of the mesh is left attached to the bowel to avoid the risk of injury during dissection.

The surgeon has developed the retro-rectus plane laterally and mobilized the stoma, reducing the hernia sac. The next step involves releasing the transverse abdominis muscle to achieve further lateralization of the stoma. This involves dissecting between the internal oblique and transverse muscles, avoiding denervation of the central abdominal musculature.

The surgeon performs a transsection and release of the transverse abdominal muscle, allowing further lateral dissection in the preperitoneal plane or the plane ventral to the transversalis fascia. The dissection is extended quarterly to the stoma to ensure proper overlap.

The surgeon prepares to lateralize the stoma using sutures in a robotic poly repair, or by applying pressure with the mesh onto the bowel in a laparoscopic Sugarbaker repair, which may increase the risk of mesh erosion.

The surgeon closes the defect using a zero V-lock continuous suture. A cut-to-size polypropylene mesh is placed over the defect and fixed with sutures, ensuring a loose attachment to minimize pressure on the bowel and reduce the risk of mesh erosion.
The surgeon uses an IIO V-lock suture to close the posterior layer, completing the final step of the hernia repair procedure.
